channel.title: synesis
channel.description: AI research, engineering, and the occasional philosophical aside.

item.title: World History
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Originally written in 2014. Revised April 2026.

2050 — Esosense

Augmented reality had become widespread decades earlier, replacing traffic lights, building signs, and eventually most of the visual and auditory signage humans once relied on to navigate the world. But by 2050, the wearables that delivered it were no longer enough. A “naked” person simply could not survive in the brave new world.

A new device was born: an organic implant, inserted into newborns by legal mandate. Its bio circuitry would grow in stages with the human body, eventually becoming an integral part of the sensory system: sight, hearing, spatial awareness, communication, all mediated through a single substrate.

It was called Esosense.

2090 — The Collapse

The global clandestine monitoring system MERIDIAN was hacked. It was never determined who was behind it or how long it had been going on. The hackers exploited everything the system had collected: personal communications, financial transactions, internal records of every government and corporation, control systems for critical infrastructure, and, thanks to Esosense, the physical location of every human being on the planet.

The result was catastrophic. Financial and political systems were manipulated for the gain of a few. Economies were decimated. Riots and wars erupted. Half the world’s population was gone.

2110 — The Federated Nations

Two decades later, the surviving geopolitical entities formed a central governance body, the Federated Nations (FN), with a single founding mandate: complete privacy protection. Never again would centralized surveillance be possible.

Esosense, the very technology that had made the catastrophe possible, became the foundation of the solution. At implantation, a public/private key pair would be embedded deep inside each newborn’s bio circuitry. The public key would be broadcast to nearby Esosense hosts and propagated peer-to-peer across the global mesh, with no central registry and no single point of compromise. Within hours of birth, every living person’s Esosense would hold the new key. The key would become a person’s identity for life, and the ultimate mechanism for controlling who could observe them.

2120 — Privacy Rights Management

The system that emerged was called PRM (Privacy Rights Management). It worked like this:

Every person’s Esosense continuously broadcast an encrypted “bill of rights” over short range, specifying their willingness to be observed: visually, acoustically, by smell, by touch. The broadcast carried an ephemeral proximity token rather than a stable identifier, so passive listeners could not build long-term maps of who was near whom. Once consent was resolved, the receiver’s Esosense mediated all sensory input at the signal stage, before conscious perception. If person A did not consent to being seen by person B, then B’s Esosense substituted A’s face with a plausible but non-persistent placeholder before the image ever reached B’s visual cortex. B would perceive a person, not an uncanny shape, but would have no stable features to remember and no way to re-identify A on a later encounter. The same filtering applied to every sense.

The processing happened entirely inside the head, powered by biological computation circuits that had matured over the preceding century, vastly different in form from their silicon forebears.

Indirect observation (cameras, microphones, DNA profiling) followed the same logic. The observing device would decrypt, check consent, and anonymize at the point of capture. No non-sanctioned observation would ever be recorded or transmitted. Legacy equipment from the pre-FN era was a separate problem with a simpler solution: it had been decommissioned during the regime’s first decade, and the networks that once transmitted its output no longer existed.

The attack surface was not ignored. A peer-to-peer authentication protocol verified that every Esosense instance and every observing device was running a genuine, untampered PRM implementation. Any detected tampering triggered an immediate response: for devices manufactured under the new regime, that response was self-termination.

For devices, this meant shutdown. For Esosense, which by 2120 was an inseparable part of the human nervous system, the implications were left unspoken in the FN’s published specifications. But everyone understood.

2130 — The Last Loophole

A subtler problem remained: DNA. Every human body sheds cells constantly, and a century of progress in genetics had made it trivial to identify someone from a stray skin cell. PRM could mediate what you saw and heard, but it could not follow every cell that left your body.

The solution was biological. A cellular mechanism was engineered to destroy every identifying biomolecule in any cell that detached from the body. The trigger was loss of a continuous signaling molecule maintained only in living, perfused tissue. Once a cell was shed and the signal dropped, a cascade of nucleases and proteases would activate, cutting DNA and RNA into unidentifiable fragments and breaking proteins down to their constituent amino acids within minutes. What remained was generic biochemical residue. Your biology, like your face and voice, had become private by default.

2150 — The Unasked Question

By 2150, a generation had grown up under PRM. They had never seen an unfiltered face without consent. They had never been observed without choosing to be. Privacy was no longer a right to be defended. It had become the substrate of experience itself.

But an observation circulated in certain corners, never quite spoken aloud in polite company. The protocol was peer-to-peer; the keys were distributed; no data center held the record of who was who. What had been centralized was subtler: a single implementation, installed in every nervous system at birth, held uniform by a kill switch that made non-compliance fatal. Whoever controlled the specification controlled eight billion nervous systems. Was this privacy, or was it the most complete form of control ever devised?

The FN’s answer, when pressed, was always the same: the system is open. The protocol is auditable. The keys are distributed. No one entity controls it.

That was true, for the moment. But the architecture offered no guarantee it would remain true. One could read the protocol. One could not opt out of it. The physical world had been stripped bare decades ago: no signs, no signals, no navigable infrastructure visible to the unaugmented eye. A person without Esosense could not cross a street, enter a building, or be recognized as a citizen. And a person whose Esosense detected tampering would not survive at all.

The architects of PRM had set out to build the opposite of MERIDIAN. Whether they had succeeded, or merely built its mirror image, was a question the system itself was not designed to answer.

To be continued…

item.title: AbstRaL: Augmenting LLMs’ Reasoning by Reinforcing Abstract Thinking
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

AbstRaL: Augmenting LLMs’ Reasoning by Reinforcing Abstract Thinking Silin Gao, Antoine Bosselut, Samy Bengio, Emmanuel Abbe

Gao et al. [1] train large language models (LLMs) to first translate a math word problem into a symbolic abstraction, then derive the answer via external symbolic tools, with reinforcement learning rewards on both abstraction quality and final answer correctness. The paper was accepted at ICLR 2026. The reported gains on GSM-Symbolic [2] and GSM-Plus [3] are largest for the smallest models tested (0.5B parameters), where the underlying reasoning was most fragile to surface-form perturbations.

Abstract: Recent studies have shown that large language models (LLMs), especially smaller ones, often lack robustness in grade school math (GSM) reasoning. In particular, they tend to experience performance drops when faced with distribution shifts, such as changes to numerical or nominal variables, or insertions of distracting clauses. A possible strategy to address this involves generating synthetic data to further “instantiate” reasoning problems on potential variations. In this work, we instead focus on the strategy of “abstracting” reasoning problems. This not only helps counteract distribution shifts but also facilitates the connection to symbolic tools for deriving solutions. Focusing on GSM, we find that this abstraction process is better acquired through reinforcement learning (RL) than just supervised fine-tuning, which often fails to produce faithful abstractions. Our method, AbstRaL — which promotes abstract reasoning in LLMs using RL on granular abstraction data — significantly mitigates performance degradation on recent GSM perturbation benchmarks. Besides, improving GSM robustness via AbstRaL is shown to also implicitly benefit LLMs’ capabilities on OOD mathematical and general reasoning tasks, indicating that abstract thinking broadly enables better generalizability.

Method

Training happens in two stages. The first stage is supervised fine-tuning on a dataset the authors call GranulAR, where each example shows the model a four-step pipeline for solving a math word problem:

  1. Spot the inputs. Find the numbers in the problem and replace each with a placeholder name like in0, in1, in2.
  2. Reason in placeholders. Rewrite the solution chain using those placeholders instead of the actual numbers, with arithmetic steps marked inside double angle brackets (<<in0 * in1 = out0>>).
  3. Extract the abstraction. A simple script pulls those bracketed steps out of the chain. Together they form the abstraction: the equations with placeholder variables, stripped of any specific numbers.
  4. Plug in numbers. An off-the-shelf math solver (SymPy) takes the abstraction plus the actual input values and produces the final answer.

The GranulAR examples are produced by a teacher model (Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct) doing format conversion on existing GSM8K data. The teacher does not generate new mathematical content; it takes the original gold socratic CoT answer and rewrites it twice. The first pass replaces concrete numbers with abstract symbols. The second converts the socratic format into the GranulAR sub-question decomposition. Each rewriting step is filtered: the rewritten output is parsed for <<...>> derivations, run through SymPy, and only kept if the final answer matches GSM8K’s gold answer. Of 7473 training samples, 6386 (85.5%) survive both filter passes. The filter is execution-based, so an accidentally-right abstraction with the wrong structure could pass. That is presumably why the symbolic-distance RL reward exists downstream, to push the model away from accidentally-right toward structurally-correct.

Supervised fine-tuning alone gets a model to produce output in this format, but the paper finds it often writes abstractions that look right yet fail when the solver tries to run them. The second stage uses reinforcement learning (Group Relative Policy Optimization, or GRPO, the same algorithm DeepSeek used for its reasoning models) to fix that, with two hand-designed rewards:

  • Answer-correctness reward. Run the model’s abstraction through SymPy. If the result matches the known answer, the model gets a fixed positive reward. Otherwise zero.
  • Symbolic-distance reward. Compare the model’s abstraction to a gold abstraction piece by piece. The more editing needed to turn one into the other, the lower the reward. The closer the match, the higher.

The symbolic-distance reward prevents shortcut learning: even an abstraction that happens to produce the correct answer by accident receives zero reward when its structure differs from the gold reference. The gradient pushes the model to produce faithful abstractions, which is the failure mode of supervised fine-tuning that the paper identifies.

Results

The paper tests LLMs from 0.5B to 7B parameters. The headline numbers come from two endpoints: Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-Math-7B-Instruct. The benchmarks are GSM-Symbolic [2] (100 GSM8K templates, where names and numbers are perturbed) and GSM-Plus [3] (numerical, phrasing, and distractor variations of 1,319 GSM8K problems).

Selected columns from Table 1 of the paper [1]. Vary Both is GSM-Symbolic accuracy when both numbers and names are perturbed. is the percentage drop from the original 100 GSM problems (lower is better; negative means higher than original). Distract is GSM-Plus accuracy when distractor sentences are inserted.
Method 0.5B Vary Both 0.5B Δ 0.5B Distract 7B Vary Both 7B Δ 7B Distract
CoT-8S 34.0 10.6 22.7 87.7 7.73 76.3
PoT-8S 27.0 12.8 19.4 86.7 4.77 75.1
CoT-RL 32.3 7.77 15.2 88.1 8.21 81.5
CoA 28.7 0.97 12.7 69.7 1.83 57.1
AoT 27.8 4.21 12.7 70.4 2.17 56.6
SyReLM 36.8 5.54 21.1 87.7 2.56 76.0
AbstRaL 44.6 −1.27 36.5 90.2 0.86 82.3

On GSM-Symbolic’s hardest split, “Vary Both”, where both numbers and names in each problem are perturbed simultaneously:

  • Qwen2.5-0.5B: AbstRaL scores 44.6%, against 32.3% for CoT-RL and 36.8% for the next-best baseline (SyReLM). Remarkably, the tiny model scores higher on the perturbed variants than on the original problems ( = −1.3%), something no baseline achieves.
  • Qwen2.5-Math-7B: AbstRaL is 90.2%, against 88.1% for CoT-RL. The absolute gain is modest, but the drop-from-original () is 0.9% for AbstRaL versus 8.2% for CoT-RL.

The largest per-task win is on distractor insertion, where the problem contains irrelevant sentences the model must ignore. On the 0.5B model, AbstRaL scores 36.5% versus the best CoT baseline at 22.7%.

Ablations confirm the abstract-reasoning behavior lives in the model weights, not the tools:

  • Removing SymPy and the regex extractor costs about 1 point on Qwen-0.5B and 0.2 points on Qwen-7B. The external tools are not the source of the gain.
  • Removing reinforcement learning entirely drops Vary Both to 29.6 (Qwen-0.5B) and 78.5 (Qwen-7B), below every CoT-trained baseline (CoT-8S, CoT-RL, SyReLM) and far below AbstRaL.
  • Removing just the symbolic-distance reward costs around 7 points on both model sizes. That single reward term accounts for roughly half the RL gain.
  • Removing the four-step GranulAR data format is most costly on the distractor split (Qwen-7B drops from 82.3 to 66.8). The granular decomposition is what teaches the model to ignore irrelevant sentences.

Out-of-distribution generalization. Even though AbstRaL is trained only on GSM8K, it transfers to eleven other math benchmarks (MATH, Minerva-MATH, SAT-Math, AIME-24, SVAMP, ASDiv, MAWPS, Gaokao-2023, TABMWP, College-Math, AQUA) and beats the baselines on almost all of them. On Qwen-7B, the Minerva-MATH score actually exceeds the model’s own prior fine-tuned checkpoint (34.6 → 38.6), meaning AbstRaL training improves a held-out benchmark beyond the model’s starting capability.

My take

  • The “SFT memorizes, RL generalizes” pattern keeps accumulating evidence. Chu et al. (2025) [4] demonstrated this on an arithmetic card game and a visual navigation environment: reinforcement-learning-trained models held up on out-of-distribution variants where supervised fine-tuning collapsed to memorization. AbstRaL reproduces the split at finer grain. Supervised fine-tuning yields unfaithful abstractions, reinforcement learning yields faithful ones. When the post-training objective is teaching a behavior rather than memorizing facts, reinforcement learning preserves the underlying capability. The consistency across such dissimilar tasks makes the pattern harder to dismiss.
  • AbstRaL’s separation makes abstraction itself a trainable target. Program of Thoughts (PoT) [5] and Program-Aided Language Models (PAL) [6] have the model generate Python code that an interpreter executes; abstraction and execution are fused into one program. AbstRaL splits them: the model generates only the symbolic equations, and SymPy derives the answer. The genuinely new affordance is not inspectability (PoT code is also inspectable) but separately rewardable abstraction. With AbstRaL the abstraction step has its own training signal (the symbolic-distance reward to the gold abstraction), so reinforcement learning can push faithfulness independently of execution. PoT and PAL can only reward the final answer, so a Python program that lands on the right number for wrong-shaped reasons gets reinforced.
  • The SFT-to-RL swap on an established recipe is the real comparison. Chain-of-Abstraction (CoA) [7] was Silin Gao’s prior work, the same first author. It fine-tuned LLMs to decode abstract reasoning chains before calling domain tools to fill them in. AbstRaL keeps that abstract-then-tool-call paradigm but replaces supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning, and outperforms CoA on the paper’s benchmarks. Because the authorship and recipe are held constant, the gain isolates the post-training objective itself rather than confounding it with architecture or prompting choices. The follow-up worth running: at what model scale does the gap close? If base models internalize abstraction during pretraining, the SFT-then-tools formulation may catch up at larger sizes.
  • Gains concentrate at small scale; frontier evidence is missing. On Qwen2.5-0.5B, AbstRaL lifts Vary-Both accuracy from 32.3 (CoT-RL baseline) to 44.6 and fully eliminates the perturbation gap (). On Qwen2.5-Math-7B the same recipe lifts Vary-Both from 88.1 to 90.2, a 2-point gain on a baseline already near-saturated. Some of this is ceiling effect, but the paper’s claim that “abstract thinking broadly enables better generalizability” rests on improvements observed at 0.5B-7B without testing at the 30B-70B+ scales where frontier reasoners now operate. Whether the abstraction-specific reward buys anything beyond what a larger base model already internalizes during pretraining is an open empirical question the paper invites but does not answer.

References

[1] Gao, Silin, Antoine Bosselut, Samy Bengio, and Emmanuel Abbe. “AbstRaL: Augmenting LLMs’ Reasoning by Reinforcing Abstract Thinking.” International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR), 2026. arXiv:2506.07751. https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.07751

[2] Mirzadeh, Iman, Keivan Alizadeh, Hooman Shahrokhi, Oncel Tuzel, Samy Bengio, and Mehrdad Farajtabar. “GSM-Symbolic: Understanding the Limitations of Mathematical Reasoning in Large Language Models.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2410.05229, October 7, 2024. https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.05229

[3] Li, Qintong, Leyang Cui, Xueliang Zhao, Lingpeng Kong, and Wei Bi. “GSM-Plus: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating the Robustness of LLMs as Mathematical Problem Solvers.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2402.19255, February 29, 2024. https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.19255

[4] Chu, Tianzhe, Yuexiang Zhai, Jihan Yang, Shengbang Tong, and Saining Xie. “SFT Memorizes, RL Generalizes: A Comparative Study of Foundation Model Post-training.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2501.17161, January 28, 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.17161

[5] Chen, Wenhu, Xueguang Ma, Xinyi Wang, and William W. Cohen. “Program of Thoughts Prompting: Disentangling Computation from Reasoning for Numerical Reasoning Tasks.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2211.12588, November 22, 2022. https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.12588

[6] Gao, Luyu, Aman Madaan, Shuyan Zhou, Uri Alon, Pengfei Liu, Yiming Yang, Jamie Callan, and Graham Neubig. “PAL: Program-aided Language Models.” International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), 2023. arXiv:2211.10435. https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.10435

[7] Gao, Silin, Jane Dwivedi-Yu, Ping Yu, Xiaoqing Ellen Tan, Ramakanth Pasunuru, Olga Golovneva, Koustuv Sinha, Asli Celikyilmaz, Antoine Bosselut, and Tianlu Wang. “Efficient Tool Use with Chain-of-Abstraction Reasoning.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2401.17464, January 30, 2024. https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.17464
