item.title: Convergent Evolution: How Different Language Models Learn Similar Number Representations
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Convergent Evolution: How Different Language Models Learn Similar Number Representations Deqing Fu, Tianyi Zhou, Mikhail Belkin, Vatsal Sharan, Robin Jia

Fu et al. [1] show that periodic number features emerge in nearly every architecture they test, from Transformers to long short-term memory networks (LSTMs) to GloVe, but only some of those models go on to develop linearly separable mod- classes.

The analytical tool throughout is the discrete Fourier transform. Stack the model’s number embeddings into a matrix, one row per input integer and one column per embedding dimension, and run the DFT down each column. Spikes in the magnitude spectrum reveal which periods the model has internalized, without the experimenter having to specify them in advance.

The specific values are a fingerprint of base-10 text. They are exactly the non-trivial divisors of 10, with concrete corpus statistics behind each:

  • captures last-digit structure: round numbers ending in 0 are massively over-represented in text [6], as are decimal-column patterns.
  • captures the next-most-common rounding regime: numbers ending in 5 are next-roundest after 0 [6], and price endings reflect this. One analysis of advertised prices found roughly 60% ending in 9, 30% in 5, and 7% in 0 [7].
  • captures parity: even-odd distinctions in pluralization, dates, scoring patterns.

Engels et al. (2025) found analogous spikes for days of the week and for months [3]; same mechanism, different modulus. A clean falsification the paper does not run: train on hexadecimal-only text and check whether the spikes shift to .

Abstract: Language models trained on natural text learn to represent numbers using periodic features with dominant periods at . In this paper, we identify a two-tiered hierarchy of these features: while Transformers, Linear RNNs, LSTMs, and classical word embeddings trained in different ways all learn features that have period- spikes in the Fourier domain, only some learn geometrically separable features that can be used to linearly classify a number mod-. To explain this incongruity, we prove that Fourier domain sparsity is necessary but not sufficient for mod- geometric separability. Empirically, we investigate when model training yields geometrically separable features, finding that the data, architecture, optimizer, and tokenizer all play key roles. In particular, we identify two different routes through which models can acquire geometrically separable features: they can learn them from complementary co-occurrence signals in general language data, including text-number co-occurrence and cross-number interaction, or from multi-token (but not single-token) addition problems. Overall, our results highlight the phenomenon of convergent evolution in feature learning: A diverse range of models learn similar features from different training signals.

My take

The paper’s main finding: every architecture in the study picks up the periodic signal of how numbers appear in text, but only Transformers and Gated DeltaNet (a linear recurrent neural network, RNN) lay those signals out as a usable map a linear classifier can actually read. The implication for building a math-capable model: pick an architecture in the geometrically-convergent group, and reach the geometric features by either of the paper’s two identified routes: train on natural text rich in number co-occurrences, or on multi-token (but not single-token) addition problems.

  • Spectral convergence is broader than the founding work assumed. Zhou et al. (2024) established that pretrained LLMs add via Fourier features, and that models trained from scratch on addition fail to recover them, suggesting the structure was earned during language pretraining [2]. This paper pushes the universality further: even the raw token-frequency distribution of the training corpus shows the same spikes [1]. The Fourier structure was apparently sitting in the data all along, not earned by any particular learning objective.
  • Can the sparse-autoencoder (SAE) result corroborate? Engels et al. (2025) used SAEs to find circular features for days of the week and months in Mistral 7B and Llama 3, and verified with causal interventions that the circles actually compute modular arithmetic [3]. Both are Transformers, exactly the family Fu et al. show consistently achieves geometric convergence. The two results are consistent. The follow-up worth running: train SAEs on a linear RNN (which Fu et al. find geometrically convergent) and on an LSTM (which they don’t), and see what surfaces. Three outcomes are informative: SAEs find functional circles in both → SAE-discovered circles aren’t a reliable signature of geometric usability; SAEs find circles only where Fu et al.’s linear probes do → the two methods agree and SAE circles really do mark functional structure; SAEs find circles in the LSTM that don’t survive causal intervention → SAE features can be spectral artifacts, not all of them are functional.
  • The “Clock” algorithm becomes testable across architectures. Kantamneni and Tegmark (2025) reverse-engineered addition in three mid-sized Transformers as manipulation of a generalized “Clock”, a helical embedding that stacks multiple circular features over a linear magnitude axis [4]. The Clock relies on exactly the geometric usability that Fu et al. find in Transformers and linear RNNs but absent in LSTMs, making linear RNNs a plausible candidate for the same algorithm and LSTMs an unlikely one. Convergence at the feature level doesn’t guarantee convergence at the algorithm level, though. Same features can in principle support different circuits. A successful reverse-engineering of the Clock in a linear RNN would extend the convergence story from features to algorithms; failure would show that even matched features can yield divergent mechanisms.
  • Engineered Fourier embeddings give a clean lever for separating architecture from data. The FoNE work (Zhou et al., 2025) directly maps numbers into Fourier features and reports data efficiency on 6-digit addition [5]. Fu et al. find that geometrically separable number features arise from a combination of data, architecture, optimizer, and tokenizer. Text-number co-occurrence is one of two identified data routes; multi-token addition training is the other [1]. FoNE’s contribution is that it pre-supplies the Fourier features as input embeddings, removing the model’s need to acquire them from training data. That separation enables two clean tests, holding feature-supply constant in each: train an LSTM with FoNE embeddings on rich natural text (does the LSTM still fail at arithmetic? if so, the architecture itself blocks geometric use of period features); train a Transformer with FoNE embeddings on text scrubbed of number co-occurrences (does the Transformer fail? if so, data conditions matter even when features are pre-supplied). Today neither test has been run.

References

[1] Fu, Deqing, Tianyi Zhou, Mikhail Belkin, Vatsal Sharan, and Robin Jia. “Convergent Evolution: How Different Language Models Learn Similar Number Representations.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2604.20817, April 22, 2026. https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20817

[2] Zhou, Tianyi, Deqing Fu, Vatsal Sharan, and Robin Jia. “Pre-trained Large Language Models Use Fourier Features to Compute Addition.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2406.03445, June 5, 2024. https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.03445

[3] Engels, Joshua, Isaac Liao, Eric J. Michaud, Wes Gurnee, and Max Tegmark. “Not All Language Model Features Are One-Dimensionally Linear.” International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR), 2025. arXiv:2405.14860. https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.14860

[4] Kantamneni, Subhash, and Max Tegmark. “Language Models Use Trigonometry to Do Math.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2502.00873, February 2, 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.00873

[5] Zhou, Tianyi, Deqing Fu, Mahdi Soltanolkotabi, Robin Jia, and Vatsal Sharan. “FoNE: Precise Single-Token Number Embeddings via Fourier Features.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2502.09741, February 13, 2025. https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.09741

[6] Wikipedia contributors. “Round Number.” Wikipedia. Accessed April 24, 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Round_number

[7] Wikipedia contributors. “Psychological Pricing.” Wikipedia. Accessed April 24, 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychological_pricing

item.title: How LLMs Actually Work: A Visual Deep Dive
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

How LLMs Actually Work by Y. Narwal is an interactive, scroll-driven walkthrough of the LLM training pipeline, from Common Crawl through pre-training, post-training, RAG, and inference, with live diagrams and inline annotations at every stage. It is based on Andrej Karpathy’s technical deep dive.

The nine sections mirror the canonical build pipeline:

  • Downloading the Internet — Common Crawl plus the filters that turn web sludge into a training corpus.
  • Tokenization — subword units, with a live BPE demo.
  • Training the Neural Network — the Transformer architecture and what each step updates.
  • Inference & Token Sampling — temperature and top-p, with a sampling demo.
  • The Internet Simulator — what the base model is, before any post-training.
  • Building the Assistant — SFT on conversations, the turn token format, RLHF on top.
  • Cognitive Quirks of Language Models — counting letters, arithmetic, hallucination — and where in the pipeline they originate.
  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation — how retrieved context shifts the prediction distribution.
  • From Text to Assistant — closing recap.

item.title: How to Be Anti-Social
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Learn how to sabotage yourself in how to be anti-social:

  • Assume bad faith. When someone confuses or upsets you, assume they have no sane reason for it; when ambiguous, default to malicious, ignorant, or amoral.
  • Read intent through your fears. Trust intuition and feeling, never challenge or even acknowledge the assumptions doing the interpreting.
  • Pivot when challenged. Steer away from questions outside your wheelhouse; treat any display of not-knowing as weakness.
  • Word questions to imply you are right. If you must ask, lead the witness.
  • Dig in. When dissent piles up, hold firm.
  • Curate the audience. Reframe interactions for like-minded supporters so they can rally to crush lingering threats to the narrative.
  • Ignore credentials. Do not research the people you speak with, unless you already agree with them.
  • Withhold grace. Especially from strangers who make mistakes.
  • Retreat into self. When conversation breaks down, do not seek to understand those you do not already understand.

item.title: The Global Edtech Boom Is Fading as Investors Look Elsewhere
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Rest of World reports on the global edtech funding collapse: venture capital is leaving K-12 worldwide as investors prioritize AI tools and workforce training with clearer returns [1]. Global edtech investment peaked at $16.7B in 2021 and fell below $3B by 2025; new founders dropped from roughly 10,500 in 2020 to 645 last year [1].

Where K-12 AI capacity is actually accumulating gets one sentence in the article and deserves more. Nonprofit Khan Academy launched its AI tutor Khanmigo on March 14, 2023, originally built on GPT-4, with a pilot reaching about 65,000 students across 53 school districts at $4 per month [2]. The product line has since expanded to a Writing Coach in November 2023, then free generative-AI tools for teachers in August 2024 in partnership with Microsoft, and most recently an integration with Google Gemini announced at Bett UK in January 2026 [2]. The pattern that displaced the venture-backed K-12 startup is nonprofit-with-frontier-API-access. Its incentives around pedagogy, pricing, and outcomes look nothing like the ones the $16B in venture capital was chasing.

The article’s most revealing line is that retention suffered from “murky learning outcomes,” which is to say that after $16.7B and five years, the sector still cannot reliably measure whether AI tutoring helps a child learn [1]. The places where AI capital is now flowing, workforce certification, professional skill prep, and B2B corporate L&D, share two features K-12 lacks: self-motivated learners, and a clearly priced outcome in the form of a job, a credential, or a salary delta. K-12’s bottleneck has never been content delivery, which is what LLMs are good at. It is the social and motivational scaffold around the content, which LLMs do not obviously replace. The collapse may be telling us less about AI than about which problems are tractable to a venture-funded product wedge in the first place.

References

[1] Bhattacharya, Ananya. “The Global Edtech Boom Is Fading as Investors Look Elsewhere.” Rest of World, April 23, 2026. https://restofworld.org/2026/edtech-funding-collapse-k12-startups-ai-workforce/

[2] Wikipedia contributors. “Khan Academy: Khanmigo.” Wikipedia. Accessed April 24, 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Khan_Academy#Khanmigo

item.title: Anthropic Economic Index
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Anthropic’s fourth Economic Index report (Jan 15, 2026) introduces economic primitives — a set of five foundational measurements Claude extracts from every sampled conversation — and uses them to revisit earlier findings about AI’s impact on work.

The primitives:

  • Task complexity — estimated human time to complete without AI, time with AI, and whether a conversation bundles multiple tasks.
  • Human and AI skills — years of education needed to understand prompts and responses; whether the user could have done the task alone.
  • Use case — work, education, or personal.
  • AI autonomy — from active collaboration to full delegation.
  • Task success — Claude’s own assessment of completion.

What the November 2025 sample (1M Claude.ai conversations + 1M first-party API transcripts, mostly Sonnet 4.5) shows:

  • Complex tasks get the biggest speedup. High-school-level prompts: 9× on Claude.ai. College-level: 12×. API speedups are higher still.
  • Success drops only modestly with complexity. 70% success on sub-high-school tasks vs. 66% on college-level — not enough to offset the speedup gradient.
  • Time horizons are longer than METR suggests. Claude hits 50% success at ~3.5h tasks on the API and ~19h on Claude.ai, vs. METR’s 2h. Attributed to users decomposing tasks and selecting ones they expect to work.
  • Country patterns follow an adoption curve. Lower-income countries skew toward educational coursework; higher-income countries toward work and personal use.
  • Occupation coverage is up but uneven. 49% of jobs saw Claude used for ≥25% of tasks (vs. 36% in January 2025). Adjusted for success rates, data entry keyers and radiologists are more affected than raw coverage suggests; teachers and software developers less so.
  • Claude covers the higher-skill tasks within a job. Claude-covered tasks require 14.4 years of education on average, vs. 13.2 for the economy overall — implying a first-order deskilling effect if those tasks were fully automated.
  • Productivity estimate revised down. The earlier 1.8 pp/year US labor-productivity boost falls to 1.2 pp (Claude.ai) or 1.0 pp (API) once task reliability is factored in. Still roughly late-1990s/early-2000s rates.
  • Usage is concentrating. The top-10 work tasks now account for 24% of Claude.ai traffic (up from 21% in January). Computer and math tasks are ~⅓ of Claude.ai and nearly half of API.
  • Augmentation has overtaken automation. 52% vs. 45% on Claude.ai in November, reversing the August split.
  • US state distribution is flattening. On current trends, Claude use would be geographically equalized across US states within 2–5 years.

My take

“Time horizon” in this report borrows METR’s framing: the longest task, measured in human-equivalent hours, that a model can complete with at least 50% success. The metric is designed to track AI progress without saturating the way fixed benchmarks do, since as models improve, the horizon stretches.

Which brings a caveat about what is actually being measured in Anthropic’s report. They didn’t actually measure the time from actual humans from what I can tell. They asked Claude:

We derive these primitives from asking Claude to answer a common set of questions about every conversation in our sample for this report.

And in a footnote:

We measure complexity through estimated human time to complete tasks without AI, time spent completing tasks with AI, and whether users handle multiple tasks within a single conversation.

So while METR’s 2h figure is grounded in actual humans on the clock, Anthropic’s 3.5h and 19h are model-generated estimates of human time. The two methodologies are not exactly the same.

Assuming the two sides are comparable, the differences in time horizon between METR and Anthropic come from differences in experimental conditions, not from differences in what the model can do (because they were all measured on the same model). Two things vary across the setups: whether a human steers the model mid-task, and whether the task was pre-selected as something the model could plausibly handle. METR has neither: an autonomous agent runs a fixed benchmark, with no steering and no pre-selection. The API sample has pre-selection but no steering: developers send Claude tasks they have already validated will work, and the calls then run on their own. Claude.ai has both: the human steers and decomposes the work as it goes, and the human only brings tasks they expect will succeed. The result? Pre-selection alone moves the horizon from METR’s 2h to the API’s 3.5h, a modest 1.75×. Human steering on top moves it from there to Claude.ai’s 19h, a much bigger 5× jump.

This is less a story about what Claude can do today than about how humans and AI work together. The 5× multiplier from human steering shows that the most productive AI work right now happens in the human-AI pair, not in autonomous agents. The complement is the point: humans bring task decomposition, judgment about what to attempt, and verification of what comes back; the model brings speed, breadth, and stamina. The 19-hour result on Claude.ai is what that pairing makes possible, on tasks neither side would have completed as quickly alone. As models get better, the kinds of work that fit this pairing keep expanding, and so does the range of jobs where the most useful human skill is steering the model well rather than competing with it.

item.title: Proof Generation vs Digestion
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Terence Tao on Mastodon:

As a crude first approximation, the problem-solving component of mathematical research (which, one should stress, is not the only aspect of such research) can be decomposed into three subcomponents:

  1. Proof generation (finding a solution to a given problem);
  2. Proof verification (checking that a proposed solution actually works); and
  3. Proof digestion (understanding the essence of a solution, placing it in context with previous literature, summarizing and explaining it effectively, and gaining insights on other related problems and topics).

Until recently, all three of these subtasks were rather difficult and time-intensive to perform; but a human mathematician (or a collaboration between several mathematicians) who had invested the effort to both generate a proof and verify it usually gained enough understanding into the structure of that proof that they could also digest it effectively. Because of this, our community has been generally content to emphasize the proof generation and verification aspect of problem solving, as the proof digestion tended to be created naturally as an organic byproduct of these first two aspects. This was also convenient for assessing proof efforts, as the generation and verification tasks had well-defined objectives, whilst proof digestion was a more subjective and open-ended process. [Though “the ability to present the result at a research conference and take questions” is a rough first approximation of a metric for whether a proof has been digested,]

However, recent advances in both AI and proof formalization have begun to vastly accelerate and automate the first two components of this process. This is leading to a new type of “impedance mismatch”: problems for which solutions can be rapidly generated and verified in a mostly automated process, but for which no human author has understood the arguments well enough to initiate the (much slower) digestion process. In fact, with the current cultural incentives that reward the first authors to “solve” the problem, rather than the later authors who “digest” the solution, one may end up with the perverse situation in which an AI-generated (and formally verified) solution to an problem that is presented to the community without any significant digestion may actually inhibit the progress of the field that the problem lies in, by discouraging any further attempts to work on the problem, simplify and explain the proof, and extract broader insights.

Perhaps having more refined terminology to describe solutions would help. For instance, a proof that is proposed but unverified should be viewed as 1/3 of a solution at best, while a proof that is both proposed and verified remains only 2/3 of a solution until a proper, well-digested presentation of the results has been made. As a concrete example: I would currently rate the recent progress on Erdos problem #1169 which has created some buzz on social media as currently constituting 2/3 of a solution in this framework: a proof has been generated and verified, but the broader impacts of the argument on the rest of the field are still being assessed. Eventually, I espect a fully satisfactory solution to this problem to be obtained, but this may take more time that one is now accustomed to in this era of rapid proof generation and proof verification.

This post from Terence on math, as always, strikes me as generally applicable to many rigorous intellectual endeavors: research in other fields, software engineering, and even writing and other creative work. Again, this is the consequence of abundance, which will elevate (whether we are ready or not) the importance of understanding, reviewing, discerning, and taste. The question is how we reverse the incentive structure to start appreciating these aspects much more, rather than just focusing on “the first”.

Originally posted on Mastodon.

item.title: Overzealous Coding Models
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

In Coding Models Are Doing Too Much, nrehiew puts a number on a frustration anyone using AI coding tools has felt — the model fixes the bug, then quietly rewrites half the function around it.

  • The problem. Ask a model to fix an off-by-one and get a full rewrite — new helpers, renamed variables, added validation. The fix is correct; the diff is enormous and review-hostile. The post calls this over-editing: structural divergence beyond what the minimal fix requires.
  • Measurement. Rather than have an LLM introduce bugs, the author programmatically corrupts 400 BigCodeBench problems (flip an operator, swap a boolean, etc.), so the ground-truth fix is exactly the reversal. Models are scored on Pass@1, token-level Levenshtein vs. the corrupted input, and added cognitive complexity.
  • Frontier results. GPT-5.4 over-edits the most (Levenshtein 0.39, added CC 2.31) while also being one of the weakest at correctness. Claude Opus 4.6 leads on both axes — highest Pass@1 (0.91) and smallest diffs (Levenshtein 0.06).
  • Prompting helps. A single line — “IMPORTANT: Try to preserve the original code and the logic of the original code as much as possible” — improves both Pass@1 and Levenshtein for almost every model. Reasoning models gain the most.
  • Reasoning defaults to over-editing. On samples where both reasoning and non-reasoning variants get the answer correct, the reasoning version edits more by default in most model pairs — extended thinking buys more “improvements” no one asked for. Claude Opus 4.6 is the notable exception, with its reasoning variant editing substantially less. Once instructed, reasoning models match or undercut their non-reasoning siblings in almost every pair.
  • Training. Starting from Qwen3 4B 2507 Instruct (and validated on Qwen3 14B), plain SFT collapses out-of-domain (Pass@1 drops to 0.46) — the model memorizes corruption-reversals rather than the behavior. RL with a reward combining functional correctness and Levenshtein generalizes cleanly, with no LiveCodeBench regression. LoRA at rank 64 nearly matches the full fine-tune.

My take

  • The Opus 4.6 gap is not subtle — highest Pass@1 and smallest diffs, in both reasoning and non-reasoning modes. Anthropic appears to have trained explicitly against this failure mode, and it shows.
  • A single sentence of prompt closes most of the gap for everyone else — over-editing is a default, not a capability ceiling.
  • Style problem, not knowledge problem. That LoRA at rank 64 nearly matches the full fine-tune is the giveaway — a small parameter delta shifts the behavior without touching the underlying capability.
  • The tooling implication is bigger than the model implication. “Preserve the original code” should be wired into every coding tool’s system prompt by default, and diff-review UI should surface a minimality score alongside the patch.

item.title: Sequential KV Cache Compression via Probabilistic Language Tries
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

The paper argues that per-vector KV cache compression (TurboQuant et al.) optimises the wrong objective — KV vectors are samples from the language the model already predicts, so the right unit of compression is the sequence, not the vector:

Recent work on KV cache quantization, culminating in TurboQuant, has approached the Shannon entropy limit for per-vector compression of transformer key-value caches. We observe that this limit applies to a strictly weaker problem than the one that actually matters: compressing the KV cache as a sequence. The tokens stored in a KV cache are not arbitrary floating-point data — they are samples from the exact formal language the model was trained on, and the model is by construction a near-optimal predictor of that language. We introduce sequential KV compression, a two-layer architecture that exploits this structure. The first layer, probabilistic prefix deduplication, identifies semantically equivalent shared prefixes across sessions using the trie metric from Probabilistic Language Tries (PLTs). The second layer, predictive delta coding, stores only the residual of each new KV vector from the model’s own prediction of it, achieving a per-token entropy bound of . We prove that at typical language model perplexity — approximately 10–20 for fluent English text — this bound is 3.3–4.3 bits on average per token position, compared to TurboQuant’s 3 bits per vector component (with typical attention heads having 64–128 components). The theoretical compression ratio over TurboQuant is approximately 914,000× at the Shannon limit. Even at 1000× above the entropy floor — a deliberately pessimistic worst-case overhead, two orders of magnitude above the 2–5× typical of practical source coders — the ratio remains approximately 914× over TurboQuant, with compression improving rather than degrading as context length grows. The two layers are orthogonal and compose with existing per-vector quantization methods including TurboQuant.

A bit of context. When a chatbot generates a reply token by token, the model stashes the intermediate state for every previous token in something called the KV (key-value) cache, so it doesn’t have to recompute the whole context on each new word. That cache is now the dominant memory cost of serving large language models — long conversations and many concurrent users will eat GPU memory long before the model weights do. So a small industry has grown up around squeezing the cache: the latest, TurboQuant, gets close to the theoretical limit of how few bits you need per cached vector.

The paper’s observation is obvious in hindsight: those cached vectors aren’t arbitrary data — they’re things the model can already predict, because the model just generated the tokens they came from. So why not let the model’s own predictions do the compression work? The first layer notices when a new conversation starts with the same prefix as a previous one and reuses the cached state. The second layer stores only the gap between each new cached vector and what the model would have predicted. Both layers stack on top of per-vector compressors like TurboQuant — which is why the theoretical headline ratio gets to six orders of magnitude.

But the scheme adds a robustness wrinkle that per-vector methods don’t have. The same forward pass on a GPU doesn’t always return identical numbers — what else is in the batch alongside your request can shift the result slightly. Thinking Machines’ Defeating Nondeterminism in LLM Inference documented exactly this: Qwen-235B at temperature 0 produced 80 unique completions over 1000 identical requests, all from non-batch-invariant kernels in the forward pass. Per-vector compression is immune: recovery is just a fixed formula on stored bits, no model rerun. Schemes whose decode is pure arithmetic are similarly safe — the wrinkle only appears when the model itself sits on the recovery path. Here, the model rerun is the recovery, so any drift between the original prediction and the regenerated one shows up as reconstruction error. Usually the TurboQuant layer underneath is noisier than the drift and absorbs it — but at positions where the model is highly confident and the stored gap is tiny, the same prompt could reconstruct slightly differently depending on how it got batched.

https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.15356

item.title: Civilization’s Three Stages, Applied to Mathematics
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Terence Tao quoting Douglas Adams to frame what AI is doing to mathematics:

“The history of every major galactic civilization tends to pass through three distinct and recognizable phases, those of Survival, Inquiry and Sophistication, otherwise known as the How, Why, and Where phases. For instance, the first phase is characterized by the question ‘How can we eat?’, the second by the question ‘Why do we eat?’ and the third by the question, ‘Where shall we have lunch?’” (Douglas Adams, “The Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Galaxy”)

Adams’ quote, whie humorous in intent, seems like a prescient description of the current evolution of cognitive disciplines such as mathematics. To use Adams’ framing, most of the history of mathematics has existed in the “Survival” phase, in which proofs were scarce and we were willing to obtain them by whatever means necessary (as long as they were verified to be correct), analogously to how a human in an era of caloric scarcity would accept food from virtually any source, so long as they were verified to be edible.

But we are now entering an era where generative cognitive tasks, such as finding a proof to a given problem, are becoming cheap (as measured per user, rather than through overall capital investment) and relatively plentiful, analogously to how the Green Revolution dramatically increased crop yields and significantly reduced the occurrence of famine. As such, we are beginning to experience Adams’ somewhat turbulent “Inquiry” phase, in which fundamental questions, such as why mathematicians even seek proofs in the first place, and what qualities besides correctness do we want from such proofs, are now being seriously discussed not just by philosophers of mathematics, but by practicing mathematicians as well.

However, at the other end of this transitional period is the “Sophistication” phase, in which our community has fully transitioned from a scarcity mindset to an abundance mindset. The objective will no longer be to accumulate as many proofs (of varying levels of quality) as possible, but to create more sophisticated experiences around curated collections of proofs: enjoyable conversations over lunch, rather than to scavenging for all available edible food sources. This will require the mathematicians of the future to prioritize a different set of skills than the ones we promote currently: “culinary” skills, such as mathematical exposition and construction of “big picture” narratives, may become at least as important as the “food gathering” skills of locating proofs.

The transition from scarcity to abundance triggers the question of selection, and selection requires wisdom and taste. This applies to many human pursuits other than math.

References

[1] Terence Tao. Mastodon, 2026-04-20. https://mathstodon.xyz/@tao/116438112449752681

item.title: Prompt Repetition Improves Non-Reasoning LLMs
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Another inference-time trick for getting more out of an LLM without touching its weights [1].

When not using reasoning, repeating the input prompt improves performance for popular models (Gemini, GPT, Claude, and Deepseek) without increasing the number of generated tokens or latency.

Why does this work? Causal attention. The autoregressive LLMs we’re talking about — Gemini, GPT, Claude, Deepseek — read left-to-right: each token can only attend to tokens that came before it. So when a model is given <context> <question>, it has to encode every word of the context before it ever sees the question. This is the opposite of how “good” students prepare for exams: they look at past questions first, then read the texts with those questions in mind, focusing attention on what’s likely to be asked. Repeating the prompt gives the model the same second pass — by the time it encodes the second copy, the question is already in view, so the context can be processed in light of what’s being asked. Because prefill is parallelizable, doubling the prompt doesn’t slow generation down.

The paper itself frames the root cause:

LLMs are often trained as causal language models, i.e. past tokens cannot attend to future tokens. Therefore, the order of the tokens in a user’s query can affect prediction performance.

Bidirectional attention over the prefix would fix this natively — every prompt token attending to every other, no repetition needed. Encoder-decoder models (T5, BART) do this in their encoder; Prefix-LM variants (UL2) bidirectionally attend over the prompt and switch to causal only at generation. The paper flags “Explore interactions with techniques such as Prefix LM” as a future direction. But decoder-only causal architectures won the scaling battle: simpler training at scale, more efficient KV-caching for sequential serving, more natural in-context learning. Prompt repetition is a runtime patch that buys back some of what bidirectional attention would natively provide — without retraining the frontier models we already have.

Compare to the LLM Neuroanatomy post: there, the trick was duplicating a block of middle transformer layers and running them twice. Here, the trick is simpler still — duplicate the input itself. Different layer of the stack, same underlying intuition: give the model another pass over the same information.

References

[1] Yaniv Leviathan, Matan Kalman, and Yossi Matias. “Prompt Repetition Improves Non-Reasoning LLMs.” arXiv. 2025-12-17. https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14982

item.title: A Rube Goldberg Pipeline for Sharing Links
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Most posts here start as a link or a paragraph I read on my phone, in Mastodon or RSS or an article. By the time I’m back at my Mac, the thread is usually gone: the link buried under three days of tabs, the half-formed reaction long since dissolved.

What I wanted, in three steps:

  1. I capture on my phone in seconds, with whatever sliver of a thought I have at the moment.
  2. I finish on my Mac at leisure, with VSCode and Claude Code.
  3. I publish to the blog AND cross-post to Mastodon in one motion, preview cards included.

What it took: half a dozen different systems chained together. It starts when a scaffolded draft lands in my repo within seconds of a phone tap. Hours or days later, when I’m back at my Mac and have written my commentary, running the publish command takes about three minutes to put the post live on benjaminhan.net and a toot out on sigmoid.social. The systems involved are the iOS share sheet, a Pythonista script, GitHub’s Contents API, GitHub Actions, GitHub Pages, and Mastodon’s preview-card service.

The result is more engineering than bookmarking really needs. Below is the contraption, and the spots where it tends to stick.

The pipeline

Each arrow is someone else’s HTTP request, someone else’s cache, someone else’s auth scheme.

What each stage actually does

  • iOS Pythonista captures the URL plus a handful of prompts (title, slug, optional one-liner, “Notes to Claude,” “is the post mine?”, “cross-post to Mastodon?”), auto-detects the source platform (Mastodon, YouTube, arxiv, Substack, GitHub, etc.) from the URL host, and PUTs a stub .qmd to the repo via GitHub’s Contents API. The platform tag tells the next stage how to dispatch.
  • /expand-draft skill runs on the Mac in Claude Code. It reads the stub’s source-url and source-platform, fetches the page, extracts og:image / og:description, suggests categories from the existing tag set, downloads the cover image (with a citation in the figcaption), and writes a STYLE.md-compliant draft. If claude-notes is set, the skill follows it as per-draft direction. If source-mine: true, it skips the framing-commentary scaffolding.
  • Manual editing happens between expand and publish. I open the draft in VSCode and write or polish my commentary with Claude Code at hand. Quick link shares take minutes. Essays can take days. The expanded scaffolding stays deliberately minimal.
  • /publish-draft skill validates the draft against the style guide, strips scaffolding, git mvs to posts/, commits, pushes.
  • GitHub Actions invokes Quarto to render the site. Quarto then runs three post-render scripts in sequence: feed-render.py rasterizes Mermaid diagrams and MathJax expressions to SVG so the RSS feed carries real images, feed-utm.py injects UTM parameters into outbound links for source attribution, and feed-paginate.py slices the feed into RFC 5005 paged archives so the index file stays small while subscribers can still walk back through history.
  • Mastodon cross-post (only if also-toot: true) waits for CI to finish, verifies the URL is reachable, composes a ≤500-character status from the post’s title and category-derived hashtags, asks me to confirm, then POSTs to /api/v1/statuses. The token lives in macOS Keychain and never appears in any committed file.

Gotchas

The pipeline above looks clean in a flowchart. It is not. Here are the bumps I hit, in roughly the order they showed up.

  • dialogs.input_alert doesn’t block in Pythonista’s share extension. The dialog works fine when I tap ▶ inside the Pythonista app. From the share sheet, it races through every prompt with empty inputs. Symptom: nothing happens, no error, the script finishes silently. Fix: switch to console.input_alert. Same signature, behaves correctly in the extension sandbox.
  • GitHub Contents API needs sha to update an existing file. I hit this on a slug collision (HTTP 422, “sha wasn’t supplied”). The script now GETs the path first. If the file exists, it offers Replace (passing the existing sha so the PUT is treated as an update) or Cancel.
  • Mastodon caches link-preview failures aggressively. I posted a status with a URL that was still 404 because CI hadn’t deployed yet. Mastodon fetched, failed, and cached the miss for hours. The blog post eventually goes live but the toot stays card-less. Fix: the publish skill now does gh run watch until CI is green and curl -sI confirms 200 before composing the toot.
  • Quarto’s draft-mode: gone strips draft pages to empty HTML. Local quarto preview shows blank pages for files in drafts/. Workaround: a _quarto-preview.yml profile that flips draft-mode: unlinked for previews. Production CI builds without the --profile=preview flag, so the deployed site never includes drafts.
  • VSCode’s Quarto extension doesn’t inherit terminal.integrated.env.osx. Setting QUARTO_PROFILE=preview in workspace settings only affects the integrated terminal. The extension spawns Quarto in a separate process tree with its own inherited env. Real fix: export QUARTO_PROFILE=preview in ~/.zshrc, then Cmd-Q and relaunch VSCode from the terminal so the extension inherits it.
  • image: frontmatter is metadata, not body content. Quarto uses image: only for og:image meta tags and listing thumbnails. It does not render an inline cover image on the post page itself. The body needs an explicit ![](cover.jpg){fig-align="center"} for the cover to actually appear.
  • .claude/ was gitignored, hiding the per-project skills. The first commit of expand-draft.md and publish-draft.md resulted in zero tracked files. The directory was excluded entirely. Git’s rule is that a re-include cannot reach a file under an already-excluded parent directory, so !.claude/skills/ does not work against .claude/. The fix is to widen the exclusion to .claude/* first, then re-include .claude/skills/.

Why bother

Three reasons:

  • The iOS-to-Mac handoff matches how I actually find good links. On a phone, in an RSS or Mastodon reader, away from a keyboard. Capturing them as scaffolded drafts means I don’t lose the thread between “I should put more thought to it” and “I’m back at the Mac.” The drafts queue up. I work on them when I have time.
  • The cross-post closes the loop. Mastodon followers see a properly formatted toot with a preview card. The blog gets the post. The RSS feed and llms.txt get the entry. One iOS tap sets all three in motion, and the HTTP hops underneath are hidden by the time I push the button, which is the part the system is supposed to handle.
  • The pipeline keeps a human in the loop. The phone capture is mine. The commentary is mine. The publish command is a manual step, not a webhook. The Mastodon toot waits for my confirmation before going out. Automation handles plumbing. Editorial calls stay with me.

The pipeline is elaborate, fragile, and a little excessive. For a workflow that runs every few days, that is also fine.

item.title: Sounds of Joy
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

The Listening Museum — an interactive page that lets you click any of 36 mechanical keyboards (from the 1985 IBM Model M to modern thocky customs) and hear it mapped to your typing.

I’ve been a faithful user of Apple Magic Keyboards, and I love their low profiles. But I’ve been obsessed with mechanical keyboards — the low-profile ones. Just haven’t found a perfect one I like.

item.title: Claude Opus 4.7’s New Tokenizer: ~40% More Tokens Than 4.6
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Claude Token Counter, now with model comparisons — Simon Willison upgraded his token counter to compare Claude models head-to-head. Headline findings:

  • Opus 4.7 ships a new tokenizer — the first Claude model to change it. Anthropic’s announcement framed it as a 1.0–1.35× token-count increase depending on content type.
  • Real measurement: 1.46×. Pasting the Opus 4.7 system prompt itself through both tokenizers gave 4.7 ~46% more tokens than 4.6, slightly above Anthropic’s stated upper bound.
  • Per-token pricing is unchanged ($5 / M input, $25 / M output for Opus). So at the same workload, Opus 4.7 effectively costs ~40% more than Opus 4.6.
  • Images are mostly a wash, with a caveat. Opus 4.7 also added higher-resolution image support (up to 2,576 px on the long edge — ~3.75 MP, 3× prior models). A 3.7 MB PNG showed a 3.01× token increase, but that was the new resolution support, not the tokenizer; a smaller 682×318 image cost roughly the same on both versions.
  • PDFs land in between. A 15 MB, 30-page text-heavy PDF showed only a 1.08× multiplier — much lower than raw text alone.

item.title: Earth Day 10K with My Daughter — First in Our Age Groups
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

Earth Day 10K race today! My daughter and I ran as a team (“Speedy Swine & Bovine” anyone?): her time was 49:36:31 ranked 11 (of 102), and my time was 55:37:32 ranked 25. We both earned our #1 in our respective age groups!

Coming from 3 days of no running due to ankle/knee problem, and only 2-hour sleep two nights ago (hooked with work), I was planning to just walk this race. But no, that type-A me just said go ahead! I did try to stay easy-ish pace, and in the end I didn’t feel too taxing. But my HR disagreed: avg/max 155/173 bpm at this pace! Terrible!

Next race is in June — time to recover and rebuild!

Originally posted on Mastodon.

item.title: Silver Bars and Language Models: Reading Babel in the Age of LLMs
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

R.F. Kuang’s Babel was published in August 2022 [1], three months before OpenAI released ChatGPT [2]. The novel is set in an alternate 1830s Oxford and concerns the British Empire’s monopoly on a magical system of translation: silver bars that harvest the gap between languages and convert it into industrial power. Kuang was not writing about AI. But she finished describing a world in which linguistic knowledge is industrialized and centralized just as our world began building exactly that, and in retrospect the structural parallels are precise enough that the novel reads as one of the clearest available descriptions of what large language models actually do.

The Asymmetry the Silver Mines

In Kuang’s novel, silver bars harvest the gap between languages. A word in Mandarin carries connotations its English counterpart does not, and that untranslatable remainder is what powers the silver. The system converts linguistic asymmetry into material force: bridges that do not collapse, ships that sail faster, an empire that holds.

LLMs run on a structurally similar logic, except the asymmetry they mine is between what any one person knows and what the system has absorbed from millions. A radiologist has deep expertise in chest imaging but limited knowledge of rare genetic disorders. A junior developer can write a for-loop but struggles to architect a distributed system. LLMs sit in these gaps, having ingested the radiologist’s textbooks and the senior engineer’s Stack Overflow answers along with most of what is in between.

The extraction is not limited to natural language. When a physicist uses an LLM to debug a simulation, or a philosopher uses one to stress-test an argument, the model is operating inside formalized reasoning: mathematical proof, algorithmic design, diagnostic logic. These are the structured systems humans built over centuries to close gaps in their own understanding, and they are now being absorbed into the model alongside the words. Language is the most visible layer of what LLMs ingest. The deeper layer is the full spectrum of how people think and solve problems.

The Hidden Labor

In Babel, the silver-work appears as refined scholarship: brilliant students in a beautiful tower translating ancient texts. The system depends on the colonized world — languages taken from peoples who will not share in the benefits equally, knowledge extracted along trade routes that are really routes of control.

LLMs have their own version. In 2023, TIME reported that OpenAI had paid Kenyan workers less than two dollars an hour to label traumatic content so ChatGPT could learn to be polite [3]. The conversational interface most users experience as helpful, even charming, was refined through labor most of those users will never see. Behind that labor sit the writers whose work became training data without negotiation, and the domain experts whose hard-won knowledge now surfaces in model outputs without attribution.

A Different Kind of Dependence

A common response to worries about LLM dependence is that humans have always depended on powerful tools — electricity, antibiotics, the internal combustion engine. The relevant difference is what kind of capacity is being delegated. LLMs intervene in the specific work humans have most prided themselves on: reasoning, synthesis, and creation.

Software engineering offers an early read. Industry surveys by 2025 showed that most professional developers had adopted AI coding assistants in their daily workflow [4]. Whether this widespread use is producing dependence is harder to measure, though the early signals point that way. Anthropic’s own study of AI-assisted coding found that delegating to the model correlated with worse comprehension on quiz questions, particularly on debugging and conceptual understanding, while using the model to ask conceptual questions and explain errors went the other way [5]. The pattern is that more delegative use thins the underlying skill.

This is qualitatively different from depending on a calculator for arithmetic. A calculator handles a mechanical task so the user can focus on higher reasoning. An LLM that helps architect a system or draft an analysis is operating in the territory the user would otherwise occupy. The more fluent it gets, the harder it becomes to tell the assisting tool from the substituting one. In Babel, the students gradually lose the ability to imagine scholarship outside the silver-work; they can still think, but the infrastructure of their thinking has been absorbed into a system they do not control.

Benefit Does Not Cancel Asymmetry

One of Babel’s sharper insights is that the silver genuinely works. It heals the sick, strengthens buildings, and reaches even the colonized populations, who receive real advantages, distributed unevenly and always on terms set by the empire. That is what makes the students’ position agonizing: they are not dupes, they see the exploitation, and they also see the good the system produces and have built their lives inside it.

The same structure holds for LLMs. A first-generation college student who cannot afford a tutor uses one to learn organic chemistry at midnight; a small business owner who cannot afford a lawyer drafts a workable contract. Dismissing these benefits would be dishonest.

The benefits also flow through a system with a particular shape. A handful of companies control the most capable models. The people who extract the most value tend to be those who already have education, technical literacy, and institutional access. The dynamic is class-based rather than colonial, but the structure rhymes: those with existing advantages compound them, and those without gain just enough to deepen their dependence. In Babel, the empire does not need to withhold silver entirely. It only needs to control who gets how much, and on what terms.

Mediation Without Visible Seams

A central theme in Babel is that translation always involves loss, selection, and power. The translator chooses which nuance to preserve and which to sacrifice, and those choices serve the institution commissioning the translation. The silver does not transmit meaning; it transforms it, and the transformation is never neutral.

LLMs mediate knowledge along similar lines. They compress, reframe, and smooth over ambiguity. They privilege patterns that were frequent in training data and underweight those that were not. When a medical student asks for a differential diagnosis and receives a confident, well-structured answer, the contested choices behind that answer — which studies were overrepresented, which demographic biases the medical literature carried forward — are not visible in the output. The answer reads as authoritative because the system shows no seams.

This is a different sort of mediation from what universities, encyclopedias, or search engines perform. Those institutions also shape knowledge, but through visible editorial processes, peer review, and named accountability. Encyclopedias have editorial boards; journal articles have authors and methods sections; an LLM output has a prompt and a response, with the entire process of knowledge construction collapsed inside the model. The mediation is deeper and less legible.

Where the Analogy Breaks

LLMs are not silver bars, and the companies building them are not the British Empire. The comparison is strongest at the level of political economy: both systems convert distributed human knowledge into centralized capability, both produce real benefits alongside deep asymmetries, and both make complicity feel rational.

The differences also matter. The British Empire held its silver monopoly through military force, and no one was free to leave. LLM companies operate inside markets where, in principle, competition exists, alternatives can be built, and users can switch away. The coercion is softer — convenience, integration, the cost of building a rival system — and that softness changes both the moral weight of participation and what resistance can plausibly look like.

Intent is another difference. The people building LLMs would largely reject the Babel framing; they would say they are democratizing access to knowledge, and they would not be wrong. The same system that concentrates power also puts capability into the hands of people who previously had less of it. In Babel, the institution also believed it was doing civilizational good — advancing scholarship, building infrastructure, improving lives. Kuang’s point is that sincerity and extraction can coexist comfortably, and often do.

Pressure Points

The analogy is not a prophecy. Babel ends in revolt and destruction; the present situation is more open-ended. The students in the novel had almost no leverage, since the silver system was controlled entirely by one institution and the only option they could imagine was to break it. The current LLM infrastructure is concentrated but not sealed, and there are at least three pressure points that, taken seriously, could bend the trajectory away from the pattern Kuang describes.

Transparency and Distributed Ownership

The first is transparency and distributed ownership. A reasonable objection is that these sound naive in a capitalist economy: why would companies that spent billions on proprietary models voluntarily open them up? They would not, entirely. But transparency and distributed ownership are not anti-market positions, and they have coexisted with capitalism before. Pharmaceutical companies operate in a fiercely competitive market while being required to disclose clinical trial data and submit to regulatory review. The drugs remain proprietary, the companies remain profitable, and the knowledge needed to evaluate safety is not locked inside a black box. Requiring that LLMs surface their sources, flag uncertainty, and make training data composition auditable would not mean giving away model weights. It would mean establishing a floor of legibility.

The ownership side is not about outcompeting every commercial lab. It is about building a public layer alongside the commercial one, the way public libraries did not dismantle the publishing industry but kept access to knowledge from depending entirely on ability to pay. Public investment in open models and shared compute infrastructure — efforts like the EU’s nascent sovereign AI programs [6] — could keep hospitals and school districts from being entirely at the mercy of one company’s pricing and priorities. Transparency without distributed ownership gives the public the right to see inside a system it cannot influence. Distributed ownership without transparency just produces another black box. The two together start to resemble a mixed economy of intelligence.

Education

The second is education. If the deepest risk is cognitive dependence, the most direct response is to teach people to think with these tools without being absorbed by them. Curricula should treat LLMs the way good math programs treat calculators: useful, permitted, but not a substitute for understanding the underlying reasoning.

Shared Language

The third is shared language. One reason Babel resonates is that it names a dynamic many people feel but cannot quite articulate: the sense that something valuable is being quietly reorganized, and that the benefits are real but the terms are not theirs to set. A framework for that feeling is itself a form of power, since a system that can be described is harder to be captured by.

The asymmetry between what individuals know and what these systems have absorbed will keep being mined; I do not think that can be stopped. What is still open is whether the extraction is governed, shared more broadly, and made more accountable than it is now. Babel is most useful here as a description sharp enough to make the trap visible while there is still room to step around parts of it.

References

[1] Kuang, R.F. Babel: Or the Necessity of Violence: An Arcane History of the Oxford Translators’ Revolution. Harper Voyager, August 2022.

[2] OpenAI. “Introducing ChatGPT.” OpenAI Blog, November 30, 2022. https://openai.com/blog/chatgpt

[3] Perrigo, Billy. “OpenAI Used Kenyan Workers on Less Than $2 Per Hour to Make ChatGPT Less Toxic.” TIME, January 18, 2023. https://time.com/6247678/openai-chatgpt-kenya-workers/

[4] Stack Overflow. “2025 Developer Survey.” Stack Overflow, 2025. https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2025/

[5] Anthropic. “AI Assistance and Coding Skills.” Anthropic Research. https://www.anthropic.com/research/AI-assistance-coding-skills

[6] European Commission. “AI Factories: European Initiative for Sovereign AI Infrastructure.” Digital Strategy, 2024. https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/ai-factories

item.title: Chain-of-Thought, by Way of 4chan
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

I didn’t know chain-of-thought could have roots in 4chan, where the lowest and highest impulses of humanity clashed to give us the jewel of “reasoning models!” [1] It also mentions Apple’s paper “The Illusion of Thinking.”

References

[1] “The Strange Origin of AI’s ‘Reasoning’ Abilities.” The Atlantic. https://apple.news/AQUmk0Ha9SsiSaF5FeWXEUw

Originally posted on LinkedIn.

item.title: APIs, Tools, Skills, and Where MCP Fits
item.dc:creator: synesis
item.description:
Read on benjaminhan.net →

APIs, tools, and skills form a rough abstraction ladder. Each one builds on the layer below it, and confusing them leads to systems that are either too rigid or too loosely defined. MCP is a different kind of thing: a protocol that crosscuts the ladder. It deserves its own discussion.

API: The Raw Interface

An API is a defined interface that lets one software system interact with another. It exposes endpoints, methods, inputs, and outputs. It does not tell an agent when to call it, what to do with the result, or how the call fits into a broader task.

In a customer support agent, the API is the HTTP interface to the ticketing system: create ticket, update status, fetch history. The API does not know why you are calling it.

Tool: The Callable Action

A tool is a capability packaged so an agent can invoke it directly. It has a schema the model can read, a name that signals intent, and a defined effect. In many systems a tool is backed by an API, but the tool adds a layer: it tells the model what the action does, what parameters it needs, and what shape the result will take.

In that same support agent, “search_tickets” is a tool. It wraps the ticketing API’s search endpoint, describes its parameters in a model-readable schema, and returns structured results. The agent can decide to call it. The API underneath does not care who is calling or why.

The key difference: a developer writes code against an API. An agent selects and invokes a tool. The tool is the API made legible to a model.

Skill: The Playbook

A skill is a reusable method for solving a class of problems. It involves judgment, sequencing, and often multiple tools. If a tool says “send an email,” a skill says “triage the inbox, identify messages that need a response, draft replies in the right tone, and flag anything that needs human review.”

For the support agent, a skill might be “handle refund request”: check the order status, verify the refund policy applies, draft a response to the customer, create an internal ticket if escalation is needed, and log the outcome. That is not one action. It is a procedure with branching logic and defaults.

Skills are where know-how lives. A tool gives the agent something to do. A skill tells it how to do a job well.

Where MCP Fits

MCP (Model Context Protocol) is not another rung on this ladder. It is a standard for exposing tools, resources, and context to models in a consistent way. Its job is interoperability: making capabilities discoverable and portable across environments.

Without MCP, every agent framework wires up tools differently. The schema formats vary, discovery is ad hoc, and switching between hosts means rewriting integrations. MCP standardizes that surface so a tool written once can be used by any compliant client.

MCP can also expose resources (files, database rows, live context) alongside tools, which means a model can see both what it can do and what it is working with. This is useful but distinct from the API/tool/skill question. MCP does not replace any of those layers. It makes them portable.

Think of it this way: APIs, tools, and skills describe what capabilities exist and at what level of abstraction. MCP describes how those capabilities are presented to models.

A Concrete Example

Consider a code assistant that can read files, run tests, and create pull requests.

  • The APIs are the filesystem interface, the test runner’s CLI, and the GitHub REST API.
  • The tools are “read_file,” “run_tests,” and “create_pull_request,” each with a schema the model can parse.
  • An MCP server bundles those tools (and possibly resources like the repo’s directory tree or recent CI results) into a package any compliant model host can connect to.
  • A skill is “review and ship a bug fix”: read the failing test, identify the broken code, make the fix, run the test suite, and open a PR with a clear description. The skill uses all three tools and applies judgment at each step.

The Boundaries Are Not Fixed

Yes, a skill can become a tool. This happens routinely. The “handle refund request” skill from the earlier example starts as a multi-step procedure with judgment at each point. But once the team has run it enough times, the branching logic stabilizes, the edge cases get codified, and someone wraps the whole thing in a single callable function: “process_refund.” Now it looks like a tool. The agent calls it in one shot. The orchestration still happens, just inside the wrapper rather than in the agent’s reasoning loop.

This is the natural lifecycle. Skills get hardened into tools. Tools get commoditized into APIs. What was once a careful, multi-step procedure becomes a reliable black box. The abstraction ladder is not a static taxonomy. It is a description of where the judgment currently lives, and that shifts as systems mature.

The movement goes the other direction too. When a tool starts failing in new contexts, or when its behavior needs to vary based on surrounding conditions, it may need to be unpacked back into a skill. A “create_pull_request” tool works fine when the agent has already done everything right. But if the PR needs a certain description format, specific reviewers based on the files changed, and a linked issue, that single tool call becomes a skill again: a sequence of checks and decisions that the agent has to reason through.

The practical implication: do not over-invest in classifying a capability as one or the other permanently. Instead, pay attention to where the judgment lives right now. If the agent is making multi-step decisions around a capability, that is a skill, even if it is technically implemented as a single function. If a capability can be called reliably without the agent thinking about it, that is a tool, even if it was once a complex workflow.

When You Are Building an Agent

If you are deciding what to build and in what order:

  1. Start with the APIs. You need raw access to the systems your agent will interact with. If the integration does not exist, nothing above it works.
  2. Wrap them as tools. Give each action a clear name, a schema, and a description. This is what the model will actually call.
  3. If you need portability, add MCP. If your tools will be used across multiple model hosts or clients, exposing them through MCP saves you from writing bespoke integrations for each one. If you are building for a single environment, this can wait.
  4. Encode skills last. Once your agent can take individual actions reliably, identify the recurring workflows where sequencing and judgment matter. Package those as skills. Premature skills, built before the underlying tools are solid, tend to be brittle.

The layers are complements: APIs provide access, tools provide action, MCP provides portability, and skills provide method. Most agent systems need all four, but they should be built in roughly that order.

Originally posted on LinkedIn.
